title: Generative AI Basics
description: Understand how LLMs work, what they can do, and where they fall short.

questions.q: What does 'Generative AI' fundamentally do?
options: Produces new content (text, images, code, audio, video) based on patterns learned from training data
Searches the internet and returns existing web pages
Stores and retrieves data from a fixed database
questions.explanation: Generative AI creates new outputs by sampling from learned probability distributions, unlike search engines which retrieve existing content.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT a generative AI capability?
options: Guaranteeing 100% factually accurate answers to every question
Writing essays from a topic prompt
questions.explanation: Generative models are probabilistic — they can hallucinate. Accuracy guarantees require verification systems, not the raw model.

questions.q: What is the KEY difference between generative AI and traditional ('discriminative') AI?
options: Generative AI creates new outputs; discriminative AI classifies or predicts from inputs
Generative AI runs on GPUs; discriminative AI runs on CPUs
Generative AI is free; discriminative AI is paid
questions.explanation: Classifier: 'is this email spam?' (discriminative). Writer: 'compose a reply' (generative). Different goals, different math.

questions.q: Which is a TRUE statement about modern generative AI models?
options: They learn statistical patterns from data but do not 'understand' meaning the way humans do
They have true consciousness and emotions
They access the live internet by default
They store every training example verbatim
questions.explanation: LLMs are extraordinary pattern matchers but lack grounded understanding, intentions, or self-awareness.

questions.q: Why did Generative AI explode into mainstream around 2022-2023?
options: The transformer architecture + huge models + massive compute + chat interfaces finally produced human-quality outputs at scale
AI was invented in 2022 for the first time
The internet finally got fast enough
questions.explanation: Each piece existed earlier; the combination (especially ChatGPT's chat UX) made it suddenly accessible to billions.

questions.q: What does 'LLM' stand for?
questions.explanation: Large = billions of parameters. Language = trained on text. Model = a mathematical function.

questions.q: At the core, what does an LLM actually DO when generating text?
options: Predicts the next token (word fragment) based on all previous tokens, one token at a time
Looks up the answer in a database
Translates the question into machine code and runs it
Asks another LLM and copies the response
questions.explanation: Generation is iterative next-token prediction. The 'magic' is that this simple loop produces coherent paragraphs.

questions.q: What is a 'parameter' in an LLM?
options: A learnable numerical weight that the model adjusts during training; modern LLMs have billions
A setting the user adjusts in the UI
questions.explanation: GPT-3 had 175B parameters. Larger ≠ always better, but more parameters generally = more capability.

questions.q: What does a model's 'training cutoff date' mean?
options: The model has no knowledge of events that happened after that date
The model stops working on that date
Users can't access the model past that date
The model gets retrained every day
questions.explanation: Training data is a snapshot in time. Without web access tools, the model literally doesn't know recent news, releases, or events.

questions.q: Which is a famous LLM family?
questions.explanation: These 4 dominate the LLM landscape in 2026, alongside open-source players like Mistral, Qwen, and DeepSeek.

questions.q: What is a 'token' in the context of LLMs?
options: A unit of text the model processes — often a word, sub-word, or punctuation mark
questions.explanation: Models don't see characters or full words — they see tokens. Tokenization is the bridge between raw text and the model.

questions.q: Roughly how many tokens does one average English word use?
questions.explanation: Rule of thumb: 1 token ≈ 4 characters ≈ 0.75 words. Code, non-English text, and rare words use more tokens per word.

questions.q: Why do LLMs charge by tokens instead of by words?
options: Tokens are the actual unit of computation the model processes, so cost scales linearly with tokens
Words are too hard to count
Words don't exist in all languages
questions.explanation: Each token = one forward pass through the model. Pricing by the actual compute unit makes economic sense.

questions.q: Which type of text typically uses the MOST tokens per character?
options: Non-English text and code with many special characters or rare words
questions.explanation: Tokenizers are trained mostly on English. Urdu, Chinese, Arabic, and code often need 2-4x more tokens per character.

questions.q: What is a 'tokenizer'?
options: The component that splits raw text into tokens (and back) before/after the model processes it
questions.explanation: Each model family has its own tokenizer (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece variants). Same text → different token counts per model.

questions.q: What is 'pretraining' for an LLM?
options: Training the model on a massive corpus of text to learn general language patterns
Pre-installing the model on hardware before shipping
Training the user before they use the model
questions.explanation: Pretraining is the heavy-lift phase — months of GPU time on terabytes of internet text, books, code, etc.

questions.q: What is 'fine-tuning' an LLM?
options: Further training a pretrained model on a smaller, specialized dataset to specialize its behavior
Adjusting the volume of the model's voice
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning takes a base model and teaches it a specific task or style (e.g., medical Q&A, brand voice, JSON output).

questions.q: What does 'RLHF' stand for?
questions.explanation: RLHF is the technique that turned raw LLMs into the helpful chat assistants we know — humans rate outputs, model learns preferences.

questions.q: Why doesn't training data include the live internet?
options: Training happens once on a fixed snapshot; live updates would be impractical and unsafe
The internet is too expensive to download
Training is illegal on live data
questions.explanation: Training is a months-long batch process. For real-time info, models use external tools (search, RAG) at inference time.

questions.q: What is the role of GPUs in training LLMs?
options: They perform the massive parallel matrix math required, far faster than CPUs
They display the training progress on screen
questions.explanation: LLM training requires thousands of GPUs running in parallel for months. Compute is the #1 cost.

questions.q: What architectural breakthrough enabled modern LLMs?
options: The Transformer (introduced in 'Attention Is All You Need', 2017)
questions.explanation: The transformer's self-attention mechanism replaced slower RNNs and enabled massive parallel training.

questions.q: What does 'attention' do in a transformer?
options: Lets each token weigh its relationship to every other token in the context to capture meaning
Listens to the user with a microphone
Watches the screen for the user
questions.explanation: Self-attention is how transformers understand 'it' refers to 'the cat' three sentences earlier.

questions.q: Why are transformers parallelizable in training?
options: Self-attention processes all tokens simultaneously, unlike sequential RNNs
They use multiple programming languages at once
questions.explanation: Parallelism = the breakthrough that made training on huge datasets practical.

questions.q: What are the THREE main components of attention math?
questions.explanation: Each token generates Q, K, V vectors. Attention = softmax(Q·K) · V. This is the heart of every modern LLM.

questions.q: Which of these is also based on the transformer architecture?
options: BERT, T5, GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama — virtually all modern major language models
questions.explanation: Since 2018, the transformer has dominated NLP. Almost every major language model is a transformer variant.

questions.q: Which is a STRONG suit of modern LLMs?
options: Summarizing long documents into concise key points
Replacing all human experts in medicine
questions.explanation: Summarization is a sweet spot — pattern matching across long text is exactly what transformers do well.

questions.q: What can image generation models (DALL-E, Midjourney, Imagen) do?
options: Create novel images from text descriptions, in many styles and compositions
questions.explanation: Diffusion models (which power image gen) learn to denoise random patterns into coherent images guided by text.

questions.q: Which of these has Generative AI become especially good at since 2024?
options: Writing functional code from natural-language descriptions
questions.explanation: Code generation is now production-quality for many tasks — GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor exemplify this.

questions.q: What's a realistic use of generative AI for productivity?
options: Drafting emails, summarizing meetings, generating first drafts of reports
questions.explanation: Drafting is the highest-ROI use. AI is fast at first drafts; humans excel at editing and judgment.

questions.q: Which is a NEW capability in 2024-2025 generative AI?
options: Long-context reasoning, native multimodality (text+image+audio+video), and agentic tool use
questions.explanation: Long context (1M+ tokens), multimodal input, and tool-using agents are the defining frontier capabilities of this era.

questions.q: What is a 'hallucination' in generative AI?
options: The model generates plausible-sounding information that is actually false or invented
The model shows ASCII art accidentally
The model translates to a wrong language
questions.explanation: Hallucinations are the #1 reliability issue. They occur because models predict probable text, not verified truth.

questions.q: Which task do LLMs struggle with WITHOUT tools?
questions.explanation: LLMs do math by predicting digits, not computing. Use a calculator tool for any non-trivial arithmetic.

questions.q: Why might an LLM give DIFFERENT answers to the same question?
options: Sampling randomness (temperature) introduces variability in token selection
questions.explanation: Most LLMs sample probabilistically. Set temperature to 0 for deterministic outputs (when supported).

questions.q: What ethical concern is associated with LLMs being trained on internet data?
options: They can absorb and replicate human biases, stereotypes, and copyrighted content
They become too polite to be useful
questions.explanation: Bias and copyright are unresolved issues. RLHF mitigates some, but not all, of these problems.

questions.q: What is a key reason NOT to use LLMs for high-stakes decisions alone?
options: They are probabilistic and lack accountability — a human should remain responsible for critical outcomes
questions.explanation: Medical, legal, and financial decisions need accountable human judgment. AI is a copilot, not a replacement.

questions.q: What is the FIRST thing every interaction with an LLM requires?
options: A prompt — the input text that asks or instructs the model
questions.explanation: Prompts are the universal interface. Without one, the model has nothing to respond to.

questions.q: Which prompt produces the BEST output?
options: 'Write a 3-paragraph beginner-friendly guide to budgeting for university students in Pakistan.'
questions.explanation: Specificity (audience, length, topic, region) gives the model what it needs to produce a useful response.

questions.q: What role does 'context' play in a prompt?
options: Providing relevant background helps the model produce on-target, accurate responses
Context is illegal in some prompts
questions.explanation: Models can't read your mind. The more context you give (audience, goal, constraints), the better the response.

questions.q: What does 'show your work' or 'think step by step' do in a prompt?
options: Triggers Chain-of-Thought reasoning, often improving accuracy on multi-step problems
Makes the model use a different font
Forces the model to respond in code
questions.explanation: This simple phrase consistently boosts performance on reasoning-heavy tasks.

questions.q: What is the BEST way to learn good prompting?
options: Practice, iterate, and observe — try variations and see what changes the output
questions.explanation: Prompting is a craft learned through deliberate practice. Iteration > theory.

questions.q: Which is a popular general-purpose chat-based AI?
questions.explanation: These five dominate the general chat AI market in 2026. Each has unique strengths and pricing.

questions.q: Which tool category does Midjourney belong to?
questions.explanation: Midjourney is one of the leading image generation tools, alongside DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, and Imagen.

questions.q: What is GitHub Copilot?
options: An AI pair-programming assistant that suggests code as you type in your editor
A backup tool for GitHub repos
questions.explanation: Copilot was the first widely-adopted AI coding assistant, powered by OpenAI models, now joined by Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf.

questions.q: Which tool is designed for AI-powered web search?
questions.explanation: Perplexity combines LLMs with live search to answer questions with cited sources. Bing Chat and Google's AI Overviews are similar.

questions.q: What is 'open source' in the context of LLMs?
options: Models whose weights are publicly downloadable and can be self-hosted (e.g., Llama, Mistral, Qwen)
questions.explanation: Open weights = you can run them yourself. Best for privacy, customization, and no per-call cost.

questions.q: What is 'multimodal' AI?
options: An AI that can accept and/or produce multiple input/output types (text, image, audio, video) in one model
An AI with multiple installation modes
An AI that runs on multiple devices simultaneously
An AI translated to multiple languages
questions.explanation: GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, Gemini 1.5 can all see images, hear audio, and produce text — all in one model call.

questions.q: What can you do with a vision-capable LLM?
options: Upload an image and ask questions about it: describe it, extract text, identify objects, analyze charts
Convert the image to a video
questions.explanation: Vision LLMs read images much like they read text — answering questions, extracting structure, comparing, classifying.

questions.q: What's a realistic use case for multimodal AI?
options: Extracting structured data from receipts, invoices, or screenshots
questions.explanation: Document understanding (receipts, ID cards, forms) is a huge production use case for multimodal LLMs.

questions.q: What is 'text-to-speech' (TTS) generative AI?
options: AI that converts written text into natural-sounding spoken audio
AI that talks back to humans
questions.explanation: Modern TTS (ElevenLabs, OpenAI, Google) produces near-human voice quality, with cloning and emotion control.

questions.q: What is 'text-to-video' generative AI?
options: AI that creates short video clips from text descriptions (Sora, Veo, Runway)
questions.explanation: Video generation is the newest frontier — quality jumped dramatically with OpenAI's Sora, Google's Veo, and Runway in 2024-25.

questions.q: What is an 'AI agent'?
options: An AI system that can autonomously plan, use tools, and take multi-step actions to accomplish a goal
A human salesperson for AI products
questions.explanation: Agents = LLM + memory + tools + planning loop. They turn chatbots into autonomous workers.

questions.q: Which is an example of an AI agent task?
options: 'Book a flight to Karachi under $400 for next Friday, then email me the confirmation.'
questions.explanation: Agent tasks require multi-step planning, web access, and tool use — not just text generation.

questions.q: What is 'function calling' / 'tool use' in LLMs?
options: The model returns a structured request for the host application to run an external function (API call, database query, etc.)
The model writes its own Python and runs it directly
The model bills the user for each call
questions.explanation: Function calling is the mechanism that lets agents act on the real world — calling APIs, querying databases, sending messages.

questions.q: What is the risk of fully autonomous AI agents?
options: They can take incorrect, irreversible actions without human review
questions.explanation: Agent autonomy is a tradeoff — more power = more risk. Keep humans in the loop for high-stakes actions.

questions.q: Which is a popular framework for building AI agents?
options: LangChain, LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, OpenAI Assistants API
questions.explanation: Agent frameworks handle the planning loop, tool registration, memory management, and orchestration.

questions.q: What is 'RAG' in generative AI?
options: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — fetching relevant documents and injecting them into the prompt before the model answers
questions.explanation: RAG = give the model access to YOUR knowledge base at query time. The most common production pattern for custom AI.

questions.q: Why use RAG instead of fine-tuning for a company knowledge base?
options: RAG lets you update knowledge instantly without retraining and grounds answers in verifiable sources
questions.explanation: RAG = fresh info + source citations + no retraining. Fine-tuning = bake behavior in, hard to update knowledge.

questions.q: What is a 'vector database' in RAG systems?
options: A specialized database storing text as numerical embeddings, enabling semantic similarity search
A database for graphic vectors only
questions.explanation: Vector DBs (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, pgvector) let you find documents 'similar in meaning' to a query, not just keyword matches.

questions.q: What are 'embeddings'?
options: Numerical representations of text (vectors) that capture semantic meaning for similarity comparison
questions.explanation: Embeddings convert text to ~1500-dim vectors. Similar meanings end up near each other in vector space.

questions.q: When does RAG go WRONG?
options: When retrieval returns irrelevant or misleading documents, causing the model to confidently generate wrong answers from bad sources
When the model speaks too quietly
When the model is too polite
questions.explanation: Garbage in → garbage out. RAG quality lives or dies by the retrieval step's relevance.

questions.q: What is 'AI bias'?
options: When AI systems reflect or amplify unfair patterns present in their training data
When AI runs faster on certain CPUs
When AI users are biased against AI
questions.explanation: If training data reflects historical inequalities, the model can reinforce them in hiring, lending, medical, and policing applications.

questions.q: What is 'AI alignment'?
options: The research field focused on making AI systems behave in line with human values and intentions
Aligning AI hardware in a server rack
Aligning AI fonts on a webpage
Setting AI to the correct time zone
questions.explanation: Alignment is THE big open problem: how do we ensure powerful AI does what we actually want, not just what we literally said?

questions.q: Which is a RESPONSIBLE practice for AI product builders?
options: Implement safety filters, rate limits, transparency about AI use, and clear user feedback mechanisms
questions.explanation: Responsible AI = transparency + guardrails + accountability + ability to course-correct based on user feedback.

questions.q: Why is 'deepfake' technology a serious concern?
options: It can create convincing fake images, audio, and video of real people, enabling fraud and misinformation
It is too slow to be a problem
It only works for fictional characters
questions.explanation: Deepfakes are a misinformation, election, and personal-harm risk. Detection tech is in an arms race with generation tech.

questions.q: What's an important question to ask before deploying AI in a sensitive domain?
options: 'What happens if the AI is wrong, and who is accountable?'
'What color should the AI's logo be?'
'How many emojis should the AI use?'
questions.explanation: Liability + recourse + auditability are the questions that separate toys from production AI in healthcare, finance, law.

questions.q: Why should you be cautious about sharing sensitive data with public AI chatbots?
options: Inputs may be logged, used for training, or accessed by the provider — treat them like public posts
Chatbots are legally bound to secrecy
questions.explanation: Most consumer chatbot Terms allow some data use. For sensitive info, use enterprise plans with no-training guarantees, or self-host.

questions.q: What is 'data residency' in AI?
options: The country/region where AI providers store and process user data, which has legal implications (GDPR, HIPAA, etc.)
Where AI models physically live in homes
questions.explanation: Many regulations require data to stay within specific jurisdictions. Major providers offer regional endpoints to comply.

questions.q: Which is a PRIVACY-FRIENDLY option for using LLMs at work?
options: Self-hosted open-source models, or enterprise plans with explicit no-training clauses
Free public chatbots with all company data pasted in
Sharing API keys with everyone in the company
Posting sensitive prompts on social media
questions.explanation: Self-hosting (Ollama, vLLM) keeps data on your hardware. Enterprise APIs add contractual privacy guarantees.

questions.q: What is the safest way to share PII (personally identifiable information) with an LLM?
options: Redact or pseudonymize the PII first; only share what's strictly needed
Always include all PII for context
PII is fine to share publicly
questions.explanation: Minimum-necessary-data principle. Strip names, emails, IDs, etc. before sending to any third-party AI.

questions.q: What does 'opt-out of training' mean in AI APIs?
options: A setting (or contractual term) that prevents the provider from using your data to train future models
questions.explanation: Most enterprise plans default to no-training. Free consumer products often default the other way — read the fine print.

questions.q: Which is a HIGH-LEVERAGE personal use of generative AI?
options: Drafting first versions of emails, documents, and content you then edit
Letting AI make all your decisions automatically
Replacing all human relationships with AI
questions.explanation: AI is fastest at the blank-page problem. You provide judgment; it provides velocity.

questions.q: What is 'meeting summarization' AI?
options: AI that transcribes a meeting and produces a concise summary with action items and decisions
AI that joins meetings as a participant
questions.explanation: Tools like Otter, Fireflies, and Zoom AI Companion are now standard productivity stack additions.

questions.q: Which is a SMART way to use AI for learning?
options: Ask AI to explain concepts in multiple ways, generate practice problems, and quiz yourself
Have AI take exams for you
Believe everything AI says without checking
questions.explanation: AI as a personal tutor is a killer educational use. Always verify factual claims with authoritative sources.

questions.q: What's a PITFALL of relying too heavily on AI for writing?
options: Skill atrophy — if you stop thinking, your own writing and analysis abilities weaken over time
questions.explanation: Use AI to enhance your thinking, not replace it. The goal is augmentation, not abdication.

questions.q: Which is a GOOD workflow for AI-assisted coding?
options: Have AI draft code, then review, test, and verify it carefully — never ship code you don't understand
Ship AI code straight to production untested
Never let AI write any code
questions.explanation: Trust but verify. AI-generated code can have subtle bugs, security flaws, or outdated patterns.

questions.q: How do LLM APIs typically charge?
options: Per million tokens of input and output, with different prices for each
questions.explanation: Pricing is per-token. Output tokens are usually 3-5x more expensive than input tokens.

questions.q: Which is a way to get high-quality generative AI for FREE?
options: Free tiers of Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT, Groq, Copilot, plus open-source models you can self-host
Asking your friend to share their password
questions.explanation: Generous free tiers exist in 2026. Combined with open-source models, you can do serious AI work at $0 cost.

questions.q: Why is generating a long response MORE expensive than a short one?
options: Each generated token requires a separate forward pass through the model
Long responses use a different model
Servers charge more for long files
questions.explanation: Generation is sequential and per-token. 1000 tokens out ≈ 1000 model calls. Input is processed once in parallel — cheaper per token.

questions.q: Which factor will MOST reduce your AI API bill?
options: Using more expensive models for simple tasks
Sending each word as a separate API call
questions.explanation: Caching is the single biggest cost lever. Many production AI apps cache 60-90% of requests.

questions.q: What is 'inference' in AI?
options: Running a trained model to generate a response — distinct from training
questions.explanation: Training = expensive one-time. Inference = cheap per-call, but you pay it every time anyone uses the model.

questions.q: Which is the BEST mental model for using AI?
options: A brilliant but inexperienced intern — fast, eager, but needs supervision and clear instructions
A perfect oracle that's always right
A search engine with verified answers
A magic black box you should never question
questions.explanation: The intern analogy is widely used by AI practitioners. Helpful, fast, but needs review on important work.

questions.q: What is 'human-in-the-loop' (HITL) AI?
options: AI systems where humans review, approve, or correct AI outputs at critical points
AI that needs human power to run
questions.explanation: HITL is the responsible default for production AI. Full autonomy is appropriate only for low-stakes, easily-reversible actions.

questions.q: Which jobs are LEAST likely to be fully replaced by current AI?
options: Roles requiring physical presence, emotional judgment, accountability, and novel reasoning under uncertainty
All jobs are equally at risk
questions.explanation: AI changes most jobs faster than it replaces them. The pattern: tasks within jobs get automated; new tasks emerge.

questions.q: What's a HEALTHY mindset toward AI in your career?
options: Learn to use AI as a multiplier on your existing skills; treat it as a tool, not a threat or a savior
Believe AI will solve every problem automatically
Refuse to use any AI ever
questions.explanation: Practitioners who learn AI early become more valuable, not less. Fearing it or ignoring it both hurt long-term.

questions.q: Which is TRUE about AI literacy in 2026?
options: It's becoming a baseline skill — like email and Excel were in previous eras
It's a fad that will pass soon
questions.explanation: AI literacy (knowing how to prompt, what models can do, where they fail) is now expected across white-collar work.

subPools.topic: AI in business and creative work

questions.q: Which business function has AI rapidly transformed?
options: Customer support (chatbots, ticket triage, agent assist)
questions.explanation: Customer support is one of the largest commercial AI markets — AI handles tier-1 questions, agents handle complex cases.

questions.q: What is 'AI marketing copywriting'?
options: Using AI to draft ad copy, email subject lines, social posts, and product descriptions at scale
questions.explanation: Marketing teams use AI for high-volume copy variations. Brand consistency requires templates + human editing.

questions.q: Which is a creative use of generative AI?
options: Brainstorming, generating image references, drafting story outlines, exploring 'what if' variations
questions.explanation: AI shines at expanding the creative search space. Humans pick the gems and add the soul.

questions.q: What's a key copyright concern with AI-generated content?
options: Legal status of AI output and training data is still evolving; commercial use should be reviewed carefully
AI content is always public domain
AI content always belongs to OpenAI
AI content has no copyright issues anywhere
questions.explanation: Copyright law for AI is unsettled in most jurisdictions in 2026. Major lawsuits are still in progress.

questions.q: Why do many companies have an 'AI usage policy'?
options: To clarify what data can be shared with AI, what AI output can be used commercially, and how to disclose AI assistance
To restrict AI to executives only
questions.explanation: Clear policies protect both the company and employees by establishing safe and consistent AI usage practices.

questions.q: What does 'AGI' stand for?
options: Artificial General Intelligence — AI matching or exceeding human cognitive ability across most domains
questions.explanation: AGI is the long-debated goal of broadly capable AI. We are not there in 2026, but progress is rapid and disputed.

questions.q: What is 'multimodal native' AI?
options: Models built from the ground up to handle text, images, audio, and video as first-class inputs and outputs
AI that only works in native languages
AI made in the user's home country
questions.explanation: Earlier models bolted vision on later. Modern frontier models (GPT-4o, Gemini, Claude) are increasingly multimodal-native.

questions.q: Which trend is making AI more affordable?
options: Smaller, more efficient models that match larger models on common tasks at a fraction of the cost
questions.explanation: Model efficiency is improving fast. Phi, Llama 3.x small, Gemma, Qwen small are remarkable at tiny sizes.

questions.q: What is 'on-device AI'?
options: AI models running directly on your phone or laptop, with no cloud round-trip
AI that requires a specific device store
questions.explanation: Apple Intelligence, Pixel's Gemini Nano, on-device Llama, MLC — on-device AI brings privacy and offline use.

questions.q: Which is a REALISTIC near-term AI development?
options: AI agents that complete multi-step tasks across multiple apps autonomously
AI that solves all of physics
AI that eliminates need for any human work
questions.explanation: Agentic AI for routine multi-step work is happening now (2025-2026). The grander claims remain speculative.

questions.q: What's the FIRST step for someone new to generative AI?
options: Pick a free chatbot (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) and use it daily for real tasks to build intuition
questions.explanation: Hands-on use teaches more in a week than passive reading does in a month. Just start using it.

questions.q: Which is a GREAT first project to learn AI?
options: Build a small tool that automates a real annoying task in your own life or work
Try to recreate GPT-4 from scratch
Launch a $1M startup before writing any code
Memorize the math behind transformers first
questions.explanation: Real projects that solve real problems teach you the most. Start tiny. Ship. Iterate.

questions.q: Where can you learn AI for FREE?
options: DeepLearning.AI, freeCodeCamp, Hugging Face, Anthropic Docs, OpenAI Cookbook, YouTube channels
Only by working at AI labs
questions.explanation: Top-quality AI education is freely available online. The bottleneck is time and consistency, not money.

questions.q: Which community is GOOD for AI learners?
options: Hugging Face forums, Reddit r/LocalLLaMA, AI Discord servers, Twitter/X AI builders
questions.explanation: Active, generous AI communities exist on every platform. Learn out loud, ask questions, share what you build.

questions.q: What's the MOST important meta-skill for working with AI long-term?
options: Curiosity and the willingness to keep learning as the field evolves rapidly
Memorizing one specific model version forever
Avoiding all new tools after the first one you learn
questions.explanation: AI changes faster than any field in history. Stay curious. The fundamentals (prompting, evaluation, judgment) compound.
